categories.name: Authentication & Authorization
categories.description: Identity, session, and access control patterns

patterns.name: JWT Stateless Auth
patterns.description: Self-contained tokens with signature verification. No server-side session storage.

python_fastapi.snippet:
from fastapi import Depends, HTTPException
from fastapi.security import HTTPBearer
import jwt

security = HTTPBearer()

async def verify_token(token: str = Depends(security)):
    try:
        payload = jwt.decode(token, SECRET, algorithms=['HS256'])
        return payload
    except jwt.ExpiredSignatureError:
        raise HTTPException(401, 'Token expired')
    except jwt.InvalidTokenError:
        raise HTTPException(401, 'Invalid token')

patterns.tradeoffs: Token revocation is hard (requires blocklist). Short expiry + refresh token solves this.
patterns.dont_when: Session-heavy apps needing instant revoke, legacy session-based apps

patterns.name: Session-Based Auth (Redis-backed)
patterns.description: Server-side sessions stored in Redis. Fast, instantly revocable, requires cookie support.

python_fastapi.snippet:
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, Request
from itsdangerous import Signer
import redis

router = APIRouter()
r = redis.Redis()

@router.post('/login')
async def login(request: Request):
    session_id = r.incr('session_counter')
    r.setex(f'session:{session_id}', 3600, user_id)
    response.set_cookie('session', session_id, httponly=True)
    return {'ok': True}

patterns.tradeoffs: Instant revocation + fast. Requires Redis. Session data per request overhead.
patterns.when: Apps needing instant logout, SSR apps, real-time apps

patterns.name: OAuth 2.0 + PKCE
patterns.description: Industry-standard delegated auth. PKCE for public clients (mobile, SPA).

python_fastapi.snippet:
# 1. Generate PKCE challenge
import hashlib, base64, secrets
code_verifier = secrets.token_urlsafe(64)
code_challenge = base64.urlsafe_b64encode(
    hashlib.sha256(code_verifier.encode()).digest()
).decode().rstrip('=')

# 2. Redirect to provider
# GET https://provider.com/oauth/authorize?client_id=...&code_challenge=...

# 3. Exchange code for token
import requests
resp = requests.post(provider_token_url, data={
    'grant_type': 'authorization_code',
    'code': auth_code,
    'code_verifier': code_verifier,
    'client_id': CLIENT_ID,
})
token = resp.json()['access_token']

patterns.tradeoffs: Industry standard, supports many providers. Complex to implement correctly.
patterns.when: Social login, third-party integrations, enterprise SSO
patterns.dont_when: Simple internal apps (use local auth), mobile-first (complex)

patterns.name: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
patterns.description: Users have roles (admin, editor, viewer). Roles have permissions. Check permissions on each action.
patterns.tradeoffs: Simple, fast. Fine-grained permission (ABAC) adds complexity.
patterns.when: Multi-user apps with distinct roles, admin panels

categories.name: API Design
categories.description: REST, versioning, error handling, and API contracts

patterns.name: RESTful API Design
patterns.description: Resource-oriented API. Nouns in URL, HTTP verbs for actions. Predictable, well-understood.
patterns.tradeoffs: Simple, well-known. Complex actions (search, batch) need non-RESTful workarounds.
patterns.when: CRUD APIs, resource-based systems, public APIs

patterns.name: Error Response Standard
patterns.description: Consistent error format across all API endpoints. Machine-readable + human-friendly.
patterns.tradeoffs: Consistent, debuggable. Needs discipline to use everywhere.

patterns.name: Rate Limiting (Token Bucket)
patterns.description: Limit requests per user/IP using token bucket algorithm. Redis-backed for distributed.

python_fastapi.install: pip install slowapi  # fastapi rate limiting

patterns.tradeoffs: Fair throttling. Redis needed for distributed. In-memory is single-node only.
patterns.when: Public APIs, multi-tenant apps, resource-intensive endpoints
patterns.dont_when: Internal tools with trusted users, simple single-instance apps

categories.name: Database & ORM
categories.description: Schema design, migrations, query patterns, and ORM best practices

patterns.name: Schema Migration Pattern
patterns.description: Track schema changes as versioned migration files. Apply forward and backward.

python.snippet:
# migrations/001_create_users.py
from alembic import op
import sqlalchemy as sa

revision = '001'
down_revision = None

def upgrade():
    op.create_table('users',
        sa.Column('id', sa.Integer(), primary_key=True),
        sa.Column('email', sa.String(255), unique=True, nullable=False),
        sa.Column('password_hash', sa.String(255), nullable=False),
        sa.Column('created_at', sa.DateTime(), server_default=sa.func.now()),
    )
    op.create_index('ix_users_email', 'users', ['email'])

def downgrade():
    op.drop_index('ix_users_email', 'users')
    op.drop_table('users')

# Run: alembic upgrade head
# Rollback: alembic downgrade -1

patterns.tradeoffs: Safe production changes. Migration conflicts in team require coordination.

patterns.name: Repository Pattern + ORM
patterns.description: Abstract data access behind repository interfaces. Testable without real DB.
patterns.tradeoffs: Testable, clean separation. Adds abstraction layer.
patterns.dont_when: Simple CRUD, prototypes, ORMs used directly is fine

categories.name: Real-time & Async
categories.description: WebSockets, SSE, task queues, and event-driven patterns

patterns.name: WebSocket Communication
patterns.description: Bidirectional persistent connection. Server pushes events to client without polling.
patterns.tradeoffs: True bidirectional. Requires sticky sessions in load-balanced environments.
patterns.when: Chat, live dashboards, collaborative editing, game servers
patterns.dont_when: Simple request-response, one-way notifications (use SSE instead)

patterns.name: Background Job Queue (Celery/Redis)
patterns.description: Offload heavy or slow work to background workers. API returns immediately.
patterns.tradeoffs: Decouples slow work from request. Needs Redis/broker. Retry logic required.
patterns.when: Email sending, webhooks, heavy computation, report generation
patterns.dont_when: Fast tasks (<100ms), simple in-process async (use asyncio)

patterns.name: Server-Sent Events (SSE)
patterns.description: Server pushes events to client over HTTP. Simpler than WebSocket for one-way streams.
patterns.tradeoffs: Simple HTTP, works through proxies. One-way only, no binary data.
patterns.when: Notification feeds, progress bars, live logs, one-way dashboards
patterns.dont_when: Bidirectional needs, binary data, high-frequency updates (use WebSocket)

categories.name: File Handling & Storage
categories.description: Upload, validation, cloud storage, and CDN patterns

patterns.name: Secure File Upload
patterns.description: Validate size/type, generate secure filenames, store outside web root or in cloud.

python_fastapi.snippet:
from fastapi import UploadFile, File, HTTPException
import hashlib, mimetypes, os

MAX_SIZE = 10 * 1024 * 1024  # 10MB
ALLOWED_TYPES = {'image/jpeg', 'image/png', 'image/webp', 'application/pdf'}

@router.post('/upload')
async def upload(file: UploadFile = File(...)):
    # 1. Size check
    content = await file.read()
    if len(content) > MAX_SIZE:
        raise HTTPException(400, f'File too large (max {MAX_SIZE // 1024 // 1024}MB)')

    # 2. Type check
    if file.content_type not in ALLOWED_TYPES:
        raise HTTPException(400, f'Type not allowed: {file.content_type}')

    # 3. Secure filename
    ext = mimetypes.guess_extension(file.content_type) or '.bin'
    safe_name = hashlib.sha256(content[:256]).hexdigest()[:16] + ext

    # 4. Store (local or S3)
    path = f'/uploads/{safe_name}'
    with open(path, 'wb') as f:
        f.write(content)

    return {'url': f'/uploads/{safe_name}', 'size': len(content)}

patterns.tradeoffs: Simple, works well. Cloud storage (S3/GCS) better for production scale.
patterns.when: User uploads, images, documents, any file storage
patterns.dont_when: Large files (use presigned S3 URLs), streaming (use chunked upload)

patterns.name: Presigned URL Upload (S3/GCS)
patterns.description: Generate short-lived upload URL. Client uploads directly to S3 — bypasses your server.

python_boto3.snippet:
import boto3, uuid

s3 = boto3.client('s3',
    aws_access_key_id=AWS_KEY,
    aws_secret_access_key=AWS_SECRET,
    region_name='us-east-1'
)

@router.post('/upload-url')
async def get_upload_url(filename: str, content_type: str):
    ext = filename.split('.')[-1]
    key = f'uploads/{uuid.uuid4()}.{ext}'

    url = s3.generate_presigned_url(
        'put_object',
        Params={'Bucket': BUCKET, 'Key': key, 'ContentType': content_type},
        ExpiresIn=300  # 5 minutes
    )
    return {'upload_url': url, 'key': key}

# Frontend uploads directly to S3 using the presigned URL

patterns.tradeoffs: Offloads bandwidth from your server. Requires cloud credentials setup.
patterns.when: Large files, high-volume uploads, user-generated media
patterns.dont_when: Small files, simple prototypes, no cloud budget

categories.name: Resilience & Reliability
categories.description: Retry, circuit breaker, graceful degradation patterns

patterns.name: Circuit Breaker
patterns.description: After N failures, stop calling a failing service. Give it time to recover.
patterns.tradeoffs: Prevents cascade failures. False positives (brief outage → open circuit).
patterns.when: External API calls, microservices, any unreliable dependency
patterns.dont_when: Local calls, fully reliable internal services

patterns.name: Retry with Exponential Backoff
patterns.description: Retry failed requests with increasing delay. Skip retryable vs non-retryable errors.
patterns.tradeoffs: Handles transient failures well. Can mask real problems if overused.
patterns.when: External API calls, database operations, network-dependent code
patterns.dont_when: Non-idempotent writes (use circuit breaker instead), user-facing waits

categories.name: Observability & Logging
categories.description: Structured logging, metrics, tracing, and debugging patterns

patterns.name: Structured JSON Logging
patterns.description: Machine-readable JSON logs. Queryable, aggregatable. Integrates with ELK/Datadog.
patterns.tradeoffs: Queryable, integrates with log systems. Slightly more storage than plain text.
patterns.when: Production systems, any system needing log aggregation
patterns.dont_when: Development only, simple scripts, low-volume personal projects

patterns.name: Distributed Request Tracing
patterns.description: Attach unique trace_id to every request. Propagate through all services.

python_fastapi.snippet:
from fastapi import Request, Response
from contextvars import ContextVar
import uuid, time

trace_id: ContextVar[str] = ContextVar('trace_id', default='')

@app.middleware('http')
async def tracing_middleware(request: Request, call_next):
    trace = request.headers.get('X-Trace-ID') or str(uuid.uuid4())
    trace_id.set(trace)
    start = time.time()

    response = await call_next(request)
    duration = time.time() - start

    # Log with trace context
    logger.info(
        f'{request.method} {request.url.path}',
        extra={
            'trace_id': trace,
            'duration_ms': round(duration * 1000, 2),
            'status': response.status_code,
            'user_id': getattr(request.state, 'user_id', None),
        }
    )
    response.headers['X-Trace-ID'] = trace
    return response

# In any function — just access the trace:
def get_trace_id():
    return trace_id.get()
python_fastapi.install: pip install opentelemetry-api  # for more advanced tracing

patterns.tradeoffs: End-to-end visibility. Overhead for high-volume systems (use sampling).
patterns.when: Microservices, complex request flows, debugging production issues
patterns.dont_when: Monolith, simple request flows, low-overhead requirements

decision_rules.description: Primary filter: if a pattern doesn't support the tech stack, score 0
